transcript:
You're looking at a knot of glowing arcs — pink, cyan, lavender, gold — constantly flickering and regenerating around a bright, dense center, breathing rather than settling into any final shape.

Here's the thing worth noticing: nothing you're seeing is ever actually "one" curve. Each arc individually is faint and illegible — look at the envelope function governing opacity, which borrows an ADSR sound-envelope structure, attack into decay into sustain, so every line rises, dims, holds, and fades on its own private timeline. And when an arc dies, it doesn't vanish — in traceToSubstrate, it gets copied into a separate blurred layer called substrate, "the unchosen reabsorbed into field," still glowing underneath at low opacity. The bright core you see isn't one thing — it's hundreds of these overlapping timelines, half of them already technically dead and lingering as residue.

So what looks like a single chaotic vortex is actually a population of separate decisions, all mid-collapse at once, none of them ever fully resolving before the next wave arrives to blur into it.
